content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 207 • SACRAMENTO 95814 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 25, 1978

John A. Nejedly
Senator, Seventh District
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95811

Dear Senator Nejedly:

This is in response to your recent letter in which you
state that you have been invited by the West Contra Costa
Sanitary District to visit some waste disposal facilities
in Japan that are involved in alternative disposal for both
solid and liquid waste. From your letter and a telephone
conversation with your staff we learned that the Sanitary
District has invited you to take this trip because you
authored SB 650 and SB 651, which create and fund the state's
litter control, recycling and resource recovery plan.

The Political Reform Act of 1974 would prohibit the gift
only if a lobbyist were involved in its making.1/ If a
lobbyist is not involved in the making of a gift, the only
questions which arise under the Act are whether and how it
should be disclosed on your Statement of Economic Interests.
The Commission considered a similar question in the enclosed
letter to William Atwater, Chief Counsel of the State Water
Resources Control Board.

1/ Government Code Section 86203 provides, "It shall be un-
lawful for a lobbyist to make gifts to one person aggregating
more than ten dollars ($10) in a calendar month or to act as
an agent or intermediary in the making of any gift, or to arrange
for the making of any gift by any other person." Section 86204
prohibits a legislator from accepting such a gift.

Senator John A. Nourly
October 25, 1978

Page Two


The reporting question raised by your letter is whether
the trip to Japan should be considered a gift from the Sanitary
District or whether it should be considered reimbursement for
expenses. If it is reimbursement, it would not be "income"
under Government Code Section 3203O(b). That section excludes
from the definition of income salary and reimbursement for
expenses received from a local government agency.

If you will be performing official duties while on the
trip and if the manner in which you are studied are within the
of professional concern of the donor as well, payment for
the trip can be considered as reimbursement of expenses.
To state it conversely, that the Sanitary District provides
for your stay in Japan for a period that is longer than
reasonably necessary to tour the relevant waste disposal
facilities, the excess cost of the trip may in part be a gift.
Under such circumstances you would be required to report
the value of the part of the trip which constituted a gift
on you Statement of Economic Interests.


Sincerely,


Sarah T. Cameron
Counsel
Conflicts of Interest Division

Enclosure
STC:nrw

sections.question: Whether a trip to Japan invited by the West Contra Costa Sanitary District to visit waste disposal facilities would constitute a prohibited gift under the Political Reform Act of 1974, and how it should be reported on a Statement of Economic Interests.
sections.conclusion: The trip would be prohibited only if a lobbyist were involved in its making. If no lobbyist is involved, the trip should be analyzed as either reimbursement for expenses (excludable from income if official duties are performed and the trip relates to the donor's professional concern) or as a gift (reportable on the Statement of Economic Interests). To the extent the Sanitary District pays for a stay longer than reasonably necessary to tour the facilities, that portion constitutes a gift requiring disclosure.
sections.question_synthetic: Senator Nejedly seeks FPPC guidance on whether an invited trip to Japan to visit waste disposal facilities constitutes a prohibited gift under the Political Reform Act, and what disclosure obligations apply.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A lobbyist's involvement would trigger a gift prohibition; absent that, the trip's characterization depends on whether it constitutes legitimate expense reimbursement for official duties or a reportable gift, with any portion extending beyond necessary facility tours treated as a gift requiring disclosure.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present, but clear question and answer structure exists. Letter references an enclosure regarding a similar matter (William Attwater letter). Document is a direct FPPC response to an inquiry.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a trip to Japan invited by the West Contra Costa Sanitary District to visit waste disposal facilities would constitute a prohibited gift under the Political Reform Act of 1974, and how it should be reported on a Statement of Economic Interests.

CONCLUSION: The trip would be prohibited only if a lobbyist were involved in its making. If no lobbyist is involved, the trip should be analyzed as either reimbursement for expenses (excludable from income if official duties are performed and the trip relates to the donor's professional concern) or as a gift (reportable on the Statement of Economic Interests). To the extent the Sanitary District pays for a stay longer than reasonably necessary to tour the facilities, that portion constitutes a gift requiring disclosure.
embedding.first_500_words: October 25, 1978 John A. Nejedly Senator, Seventh District State Capitol Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Senator Nejedly: This is in response to your recent letter in which you state that you have been invited by the West Contra Costa Sanitary District to visit some waste disposal facilities in Japan that are involved in alternative disposal for both solid and liquid waste. From your letter and a telephone conversation with your staff we learned that the Sanitary District has invited you to take this trip because you authored SB 650 and SB 651, which create and fund the state's litter control, recycling and resource recovery plan. The Political Reform Act of 1974 would prohibit the gift only if a lobbyist were involved in its making.1/ If a lobbyist is not involved in the making of a gift, the only questions which arise under the Act are whether and how it should be disclosed on your Statement of Economic Interests. The Commission considered a similar question in the enclosed letter to William Attwater, Chief Counsel of the State Water Resources Control Board. 1/ Government Code Section 86203 provides, "It shall be unlawful for a lobbyist to make gifts to one person aggregating more than ten dollars ($10) in a calendar month, or to act as an agent or intermediary in the making of any gift, or to arrange for the making of any gift by any other person." Section 86204 prohibits a legislator from accepting such a gift. Senator John A. Nejedly October 25, 1978 The reporting question raised by your letter is whether the trip to Japan should be considered a gift from the Sanitary District or whether it should be considered reimbursement for expenses. If it is reimbursement, it would not be "income" under Government Code Section 82030(b). That section excludes from the definition of income salary and reimbursement for expenses received from a local government agency. If you will be performing official duties while on the trip and if the matters to be studied are within the area of professional concern of the donor as well, payment for the trip can be considered as reimbursement of expenses. To the extent, however, that the Sanitary District provides for your stay in Japan for a period that is longer than reasonably necessary to tour the relevant waste disposal facilities, the cost of the trip may in part be a gift. Under such circumstances you would be required to report the value of the part of the trip which constituted a gift on your Statement of Economic Interests. Sincerely, Sarah T. Cameron Counsel Conflicts of Interest Division Enclosure STC:nrw
embedding.summary: FPPC advises Senator Nejedly regarding a West Contra Costa Sanitary District-sponsored trip to Japan to study waste disposal facilities, concluding that the trip is prohibited only if a lobbyist participates, and otherwise must be disclosed as a gift to the extent it extends beyond reasonably necessary tour periods.
